content.text: I'll start by fetching the Late.dev documentation to understand their API capabilities, then use the brainstorming skill to kick off the spec/plan process.
Let me fetch the Late.dev API documentation first.

input.prompt:
Extract the complete API documentation for Late.dev. I need:
1. All available API endpoints and their parameters
2. Supported social media platforms (LinkedIn, X/Twitter, etc.)
3. All supported content types per platform (text posts, images, videos, carousels, polls, etc.)
4. User interaction capabilities (comments, likes, replies, scheduling, analytics)
5. Authentication/setup requirements
6. Rate limits or usage constraints
7. Any CLI or SDK options
8. Webhook or callback capabilities
9. Media upload capabilities
10. Draft/scheduling features
Return the FULL details - do not summarize. Include all endpoint paths, parameters, and response formats.

content.content:
# Late API Documentation Summary

## Authentication & Setup

**Base URL:** `https://getlate.dev/api/v1`

API keys follow format: `sk_` prefix + 64 hex characters. Keys are read from `LATE_API_KEY` environment variable. Available SDKs: Node.js, Python, Go, Ruby, Java, PHP, .NET, Rust.

## Core Concepts

The API organizes around three main entities:
- **Profiles** - Group social accounts by brand/project
- **Accounts** - Connected social media accounts belonging to profiles
- **Posts** - Content scheduled across multiple platforms simultaneously

## Supported Platforms (13 Total)

Twitter/X, Instagram, Facebook Pages, LinkedIn, TikTok, YouTube, Pinterest, Reddit, Bluesky, Threads, Google Business, Telegram, Snapchat.

## Post Creation & Scheduling

**Endpoints:**
- `POST /v1/posts` - Create posts with scheduling
- `POST /v1/posts/{id}/retry` - Retry failed platform publishes
- `GET /v1/posts` - List posts

**Features:**
- Schedule for future time with timezone support
- Publish immediately with `publishNow: true`
- Save drafts (omit scheduling parameters)
- Cross-post to multiple platforms in single request
- Platform-specific customization per target

**Post Statuses:** `published`, `partial` (some platforms failed), `failed` (all failed)

## Media Uploads

**Workflow:**
1. `POST /v1/media/presign` - Get presigned URL
2. PUT file directly to returned URL
3. Include `publicUrl` in post's `mediaItems`

**Constraints:**
- Max 5GB per file
- Images: JPEG, PNG, GIF, WebP (auto-converted)
- Videos: MP4, MOV, AVI, WebM
- Documents: PDF (LinkedIn only, 100MB max)

Platform-specific limits vary (e.g., Twitter: 4 images or 1 video; Instagram: 10-image carousel or 1 Reel; Bluesky: 4 images max, 1MB strict limit per image with auto-compression).

## Platform-Specific Features

**Twitter/X:** Thread items for multi-tweet chains

**Threads:** Multi-post threads similar to Twitter

**Facebook:** Stories (24hr ephemeral), Reels, multi-image posts (max 10), first-comment automation, multi-page posting

**Instagram:** Stories, Reels (with `shareToFeed` option), carousels (10 items), user tags by position, collaborators, trial Reels, audio naming

**LinkedIn:** Multi-org posting, first comments, link preview control, PDF document posts (max 300 pages)

**TikTok:** Nested `tiktokSettings` object required with mandatory fields: `privacy_level`, `allow_comment`, `allow_duet` (video), `allow_stitch` (video), `content_preview_confirmed`, `express_consent_given`. Photo carousels (35 max), video titles (2200 chars), AI disclosure flag.

**YouTube:** Title (100 chars), visibility (`public`/`private`/`unlisted`), `madeForKids` flag (COPPA), category ID, tags, AI-generated content disclosure, custom thumbnails (regular videos only)

**Pinterest:** Title, board selection, destination link, cover image for videos

**Google Business:** Single image (1 max), CTA buttons (LEARN_MORE, BOOK, ORDER, SHOP, SIGN_UP, CALL), language code, multi-location support

**Telegram:** Parse modes (HTML/Markdown/MarkdownV2), disable preview/notification/forwarding, 10 images or 10 videos per album, 4096 char limit (text), 1024 chars (captions)

**Snapchat:** Content types (story/saved_story/spotlight), 9:16 aspect ratio, 5-60 second videos, 20MB images max, auto-encryption

**Bluesky:** Hard 300-char limit per post, 4 images (1MB strict each), 1 video, threads, auto-compression, app password authentication required

## Account Management

**Endpoints:**
- `POST /v1/profiles` - Create profiles
- `GET /v1/accounts` - List connected accounts
- `GET /v1/accounts/{id}/health` - Check token validity and permissions
- `DELETE /v1/accounts/{id}` - Disconnect account
- Platform-specific selection: Facebook pages, LinkedIn orgs, Pinterest boards, etc.

**Connection Methods:**
- OAuth (most platforms) via `GET /v1/connect/{platform}`
- App passwords (Bluesky)
- Telegram access code flow
- Headless mode for custom UI

## Analytics

> Requires Analytics add-on (+$10/mo Build, +$50/unit Accelerate, +$1,000/mo Unlimited)

**Endpoint:** `GET /v1/analytics`

**Available metrics vary by platform:** Likes, comments, shares, impressions, reach, clicks, views (platform-dependent).

## Rate Limiting

Per-minute request limits by plan:
- Free: 60 req/min
- Build: 120 req/min
- Accelerate: 600 req/min
- Unlimited: 1,200 req/min

Response headers: `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`. Returns `429 Too Many Requests` when exceeded.

## Error Handling

**Format:**
```json
{
  "error": "Human-readable message",
  "details": {}
}
```

**HTTP Status Codes:** 400 (bad request), 401 (unauthorized), 403 (forbidden), 404 (not found), 409 (conflict), 429 (rate limit), 500 (server error)

Failed posts show per-platform status and error reasons with retry capability.

## Webhooks

**Endpoint:** `GET/PUT /v1/webhooks`

Webhooks track post status changes. Delivered at least once (duplicates possible), retried with exponential backoff. View logs via webhook logs endpoint.

## Queue Management

Optional recurring time slots for auto-scheduling posts (accessible via API).

## Pricing Tiers

**Free:** 2 profiles, 20 posts/month, 60 req/min

**Build ($19/mo):** 10 profiles, 120 posts/month, 120 req/min

**Accelerate ($49/mo, popular):** 50 profiles, unlimited posts, 600 req/min

**Unlimited ($999/mo):** Unlimited profiles/posts, 1,200 req/min

All include queue, calendar integration, all 13 platforms.

## CLI

Available via `@/resources/cli` (mentioned but not detailed in provided content).

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/lhalicki/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.2/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) — this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec review loop** — dispatch spec-document-reviewer subagent with precisely crafted review context (never your session history); fix issues and re-dispatch until approved (max 5 iterations, then surface to human)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec review loop" [shape=box];
    "Spec review passed?" [shape=diamond];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Visual questions ahead?";
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec review loop";
    "Spec review loop" -> "Spec review passed?";
    "Spec review passed?" -> "Spec review loop" [label="issues found,\nfix and re-dispatch"];
    "Spec review passed?" -> "User reviews spec?" [label="approved"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Review Loop:**
After writing the spec document:

1. Dispatch spec-document-reviewer subagent (see spec-document-reviewer-prompt.md)
2. If Issues Found: fix, re-dispatch, repeat until Approved
3. If loop exceeds 5 iterations, surface to human for guidance

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`
Using the brainstorming skill to spec and plan the Late.dev social media posting capability. Let me start by exploring the project context.

input.prompt:
I need to understand the Founder OS project structure for designing a new social media posting capability. Please explore:

1. How the Notion CLI wrapper is implemented - find `scripts/notion-tool.mjs` and any related skills/infrastructure
2. How `_infrastructure/gws-skills/` is organized (as a pattern for CLI tool integration)
3. The structure of an existing namespace with commands, skills, and agents - look at `commands/linkedin/` or `commands/newsletter/` as examples
4. How `_infrastructure/` is organized for shared infrastructure
5. The `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` manifest structure
6. Any existing social media related commands (linkedin namespace)
7. How agents are configured - look at `agents/` directory structure and a sample `config.json`

Return full file contents where relevant and directory listings. Be thorough - this is for designing a new capability.

1→---
     2→description: Generate a LinkedIn post from a topic with framework selection, audience targeting, and founder voice
     3→argument-hint: "[topic] [--audience=founder|technical|marketer|cxo] [--framework=story|listicle|contrarian|howto|lesson|insight|question] [--length=short|medium|long] [--emojis] [--output=PATH]"
     4→allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write"]
     5→---
     6→
     7→# LinkedIn Post Generator
     8→
     9→Generate a polished LinkedIn post from a topic in one pass. This command executes the full ideation-to-output pipeline without stopping for user input between phases.
    10→
    11→## Load All Skills
    12→
    13→Read all three skills and the template before starting any phase:
    14→
    15→1. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/linkedin/linkedin-writing/SKILL.md`
    16→2. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/linkedin/hook-creation/SKILL.md`
    17→3. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/linkedin/founder-voice/SKILL.md`
    18→4. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/templates/linkedin-post-template.md`
    19→
    20→Apply linkedin-writing for structure and formatting rules, hook-creation for the opening lines, and founder-voice for tone throughout the post.
    21→
    22→## Parse Arguments
    23→
    24→Extract the topic and flags from `$ARGUMENTS`:
    25→
    26→- **topic** (required) — all text before any `--` flags. This is the post subject. If no topic is provided, prompt the user: "What topic should the LinkedIn post cover?" Then stop and wait for input.
    27→- `--audience=founder|technical|marketer|cxo` (optional) — target reader segment. Default: `founder`.
    28→- `--framework=story|listicle|contrarian|howto|lesson|insight|question` (optional) — post structure framework. Default: `auto` (auto-select based on topic analysis).
    29→- `--length=short|medium|long` (optional) — post length mode. Default: `medium`. Map to character limits per the linkedin-writing skill.
    30→- `--emojis` (optional flag, no value) — enable emoji mode. When present, allow 1-3 strategic emojis per the template's emoji mode rules. When absent, use zero emojis.
    31→- `--output=PATH` (optional) — file path for saving the post. Default: `linkedin-posts/[topic-slug]-[YYYY-MM-DD].md` where `topic-slug` is the topic in lowercase kebab-case with special characters removed.
    32→
    33→## Business Context (Optional)
    34→Check if context files exist at `_infrastructure/context/active/`. If the directory contains `.md` files, read `business-info.md`, `strategy.md`, and `current-data.md`. Use this context to personalize output (e.g., prioritize known clients, use correct terminology, align with current strategy). If files don't exist, skip silently.
    35→
    36→## Step 0: Memory Context
    37→Read the context-injection skill at `_infrastructure/memory/context-injection/SKILL.md`.
    38→Query for memories relevant to the current input (company, contacts, topics detected in arguments).
    39→If memories are returned, incorporate them into your working context for this execution.
    40→
    41→## Phase 1 — Ideation
    42→
    43→Display: **"Phase 1/3: Planning post..."**
    44→
    45→1. **Framework selection**: If `--framework=auto` or not specified, analyze the topic and select the best framework using the linkedin-writing skill's framework selection logic. Consider topic type, audience, and content shape:
    46→   - Personal experience or journey → `story`
    47→   - Tips, steps, or enumerable items → `listicle`
    48→   - Challenging conventional wisdom → `contrarian`
    49→   - Teaching a process → `howto`
    50→   - Reflection on a mistake or win → `lesson`
    51→   - Industry observation or trend → `insight`
    52→   - Provoking discussion → `question`
    53→
    54→   Display the selected framework and the reasoning in one sentence.
    55→
    56→2. **Hook generation**: Using the hook-creation skill, generate 3 candidate hooks for the topic and selected framework. Each hook must be distinct in approach (e.g., stat-led, bold claim, question, micro-story). Select the strongest one based on scroll-stopping power and relevance. Show all 3 candidates and mark the selected one.
    57→
    58→3. **Key points**: Identify 3-5 key points or beats for the post body based on the topic and framework. Each point should be a single clear idea that supports the hook's promise. List them in the order they will appear.
    59→
    60→4. **Audience calibration**: Note specific adjustments for the target audience:
    61→   - **founder**: Practical, ROI-focused, time-saving angle, "fellow builder" tone
    62→   - **technical**: Specific, evidence-based, implementation details, avoid fluff
    63→   - **marketer**: Growth metrics, campaign angles, channel strategy, trend awareness
    64→   - **cxo**: Strategic, high-level impact, decision frameworks, industry positioning
    65→
    66→   Display the audience and 2-3 calibration notes that will shape the draft.
    67→
    68→### Ideation Output
    69→
    70→Display the plan:
    71→
    72→```
    73→## Phase 1 Complete: Post Plan
    74→
    75→- **Topic**: [topic]
    76→- **Framework**: [selected] — [one-sentence reasoning]
    77→- **Audience**: [segment]
    78→- **Length**: [mode]
    79→
    80→### Hook Candidates
    81→1. [hook text] ← selected
    82→2. [hook text]
    83→3. [hook text]
    84→
    85→### Key Points
    86→1. [point]
    87→2. [point]
    88→3. [point]
    89→[...]
    90→
    91→### Audience Calibration
    92→- [adjustment 1]
    93→- [adjustment 2]
    94→- [adjustment 3]
    95→```
    96→
    97→Display: **"Plan ready. Writing draft..."**
    98→
    99→## Phase 2 — Draft
   100→

1→---
     2→description: Full pipeline — research, outline, and draft a newsletter on any topic
     3→argument-hint: "[topic] [--sources=web,github,reddit,quora] [--days=N] [--sections=N] [--output=PATH]"
     4→allowed-tools: ["Read"]
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Newsletter Full Pipeline
     8→
     9→Research a topic, build a structured outline, and write a complete newsletter draft in one go. This command executes the full research-to-draft pipeline without stopping for user input between phases.
    10→
    11→## Load All Skills
    12→
    13→Read all three skills and the template before starting any phase:
    14→
    15→1. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/newsletter/topic-research/SKILL.md`
    16→2. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/newsletter/newsletter-writing/SKILL.md`
    17→3. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/newsletter/founder-voice/SKILL.md`
    18→4. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/templates/newsletter-template.md`
    19→
    20→Apply the topic-research skill during Phase 1, the newsletter-writing skill during Phases 2 and 3, and the founder-voice skill during Phase 3. The template is the formatting scaffold for the final draft.
    21→
    22→## Parse Arguments
    23→
    24→Extract the topic and flags from `$ARGUMENTS`:
    25→
    26→- **topic** (required) — all text before any `--` flags. This is the newsletter subject. If no topic is provided, prompt the user: "What topic should the newsletter cover?" Then stop and wait for input.
    27→- `--sources=web,github,reddit,quora` (optional) — comma-separated list of sources to search. Valid values: `web`, `github`, `reddit`, `quora`. Default: `web,github,reddit,quora`.
    28→- `--days=N` (optional) — lookback window in days for research recency scoring. Default: 14.
    29→- `--sections=N` (optional) — number of main content sections in the newsletter. Default: 4. Valid range: 3-5. Clamp values outside this range and inform the user.
    30→- `--output=PATH` (optional) — file path for the final newsletter draft. Default: `newsletters/[topic-slug]-[YYYY-MM-DD].md` where `topic-slug` is the topic in lowercase kebab-case with special characters removed.
    31→
    32→## Business Context (Optional)
    33→Check if context files exist at `_infrastructure/context/active/`. If the directory contains `.md` files, read `business-info.md`, `strategy.md`, and `current-data.md`. Use this context to personalize output (e.g., prioritize known clients, use correct terminology, align with current strategy). If files don't exist, skip silently.
    34→
    35→## Step 0: Memory Context
    36→Read the context-injection skill at `_infrastructure/memory/context-injection/SKILL.md`.
    37→Query for memories relevant to the current input (company, contacts, topics detected in arguments).
    38→If memories are returned, incorporate them into your working context for this execution.
    39→
    40→## Phase 1 — Research
    41→
    42→Display: **"Phase 1/3: Researching [topic]..."**
    43→
    44→Execute the full research process per the topic-research skill:
    45→
    46→1. **Formulate queries**: Generate 5-8 targeted web search queries based on the topic. Include:
    47→   - 1-2 general web queries for broad coverage, news, and announcements
    48→   - 1-2 `site:github.com` queries for trending repos, releases, and open-source activity (only if `github` is in `--sources`)
    49→   - 1-2 `site:reddit.com` queries for community discussions, opinions, and sentiment (only if `reddit` is in `--sources`)
    50→   - 1 `site:quora.com` query for Q&A discussions and expert perspectives (only if `quora` is in `--sources`)
    51→   - 1 query targeting official blogs, changelogs, or release notes
    52→
    53→2. **Execute searches**: Run each query using the WebSearch tool. Extract top results from each. If a search fails or returns no results, skip it and continue — never halt the pipeline for a single failed search.
    54→
    55→3. **Extract findings**: Parse each result into a structured finding:
    56→   - **title**: The result title
    57→   - **source**: Publication or site name
    58→   - **url**: Full URL
    59→   - **summary**: 1-2 sentence summary of the finding's relevance
    60→   - **date**: Publication date if available, otherwise "Unknown"
    61→   - **source_type**: One of `official-release`, `blog-post`, `github-repo`, `community-discussion`, `tutorial`
    62→
    63→4. **Score and sort**: Score each finding:
    64→   - **relevance_score** (0.0-1.0): 1.0 for exact topic matches, 0.7+ for closely related, 0.4-0.6 for tangential, below 0.4 for loosely related
    65→   - **recency_score** (0.0-1.0): Within 7 days = 1.0, within 14 days = 0.7, within 30 days = 0.4, older or unknown = 0.2
    66→   - **combined_score** = relevance_score x 0.6 + recency_score x 0.4
    67→   - Sort by combined_score descending
    68→
    69→5. **Deduplicate**: Merge findings covering the same event or announcement. Keep the highest-scored version as primary and note alternate sources.
    70→
    71→6. **Identify themes**: Extract 3-5 key themes that emerge across multiple findings.
    72→
    73→### Research Output
    74→
    75→Display a research summary:
    76→
    77→```
    78→## Phase 1 Complete: Research Summary
    79→
    80→**Topic**: [topic]
    81→**Date range**: Last [N] days (since [start date])
    82→**Sources searched**: [list]
    83→**Queries run**: [count]
    84→**Findings**: [total] ([count] after deduplication)
    85→
    86→### Top Findings
    87→
    88→| # | Score | Title | Source | Type | Date |
    89→|---|-------|-------|--------|------|------|
    90→| 1 | [score] | [title] | [source] | [type] | [date] |
    91→| 2 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
    92→[top 10 findings]
    93→
    94→### Key Themes
    95→1. **[Theme]** — [description]
    96→2. **[Theme]** — [description]
    97→3. **[Theme]** — [description]
    98→```
    99→
   100→Display: **"Research complete. Proceeding to outline..."**

1→---
     2→name: LinkedIn Writing
     3→description: "Generates LinkedIn posts optimized for platform engagement and formatting best practices. Activates when the user wants to write, draft, or create LinkedIn content, or asks 'help me post about [topic] on LinkedIn.' Covers post structure, formatting rules, hashtag strategy, and engagement-driven writing patterns."
     4→globs:
     5→  - "commands/linkedin-*.md"
     6→---
     7→
     8→# LinkedIn Writing
     9→
    10→Generate structured, engaging LinkedIn posts that follow platform best practices and maximize engagement. Used by: `/founder-os:linkedin:post` (single post generation), `/founder-os:linkedin:variations` (multiple variations from one topic), and `/founder-os:linkedin:from-doc` (post extraction from long-form content).
    11→
    12→## Purpose and Context
    13→
    14→Transform raw ideas, topics, documents, or briefs into LinkedIn posts that a founder can review, lightly edit, and publish directly. Every post must follow one of seven proven frameworks, respect LinkedIn's formatting constraints, target a specific audience segment, and land within the selected length mode. Use the framework templates at `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/linkedin/linkedin-writing/references/post-frameworks.md` as the structural scaffold for every post.
    15→
    16→---
    17→
    18→## Post Frameworks
    19→
    20→Select the framework that best matches the topic, audience, and intent. Each framework has a distinct structure, rhythm, and engagement profile.
    21→
    22→| Framework | Best When | Default Length |
    23→|-----------|-----------|---------------|
    24→| Story | Sharing a personal narrative with a business lesson | Medium |
    25→| Listicle | Delivering multiple tips, insights, or observations | Medium-Long |
    26→| Contrarian Take | Challenging conventional wisdom or popular opinion | Short-Medium |
    27→| How-To | Teaching a step-by-step practical process | Medium-Long |
    28→| Personal Lesson | Showing vulnerability paired with insight | Medium |
    29→| Industry Insight | Analyzing a trend with data or evidence | Medium-Long |
    30→| Question-Led | Opening discussion with a provocative question | Short-Medium |
    31→
    32→### Framework Selection Logic
    33→
    34→When no framework is specified, select based on these signals:
    35→
    36→1. **Topic contains a personal experience or anecdote** -- use Story or Personal Lesson.
    37→2. **Topic involves numbered items, tips, or a collection of insights** -- use Listicle.
    38→3. **Topic challenges a widely held belief** -- use Contrarian Take.
    39→4. **Topic teaches a specific process or method** -- use How-To.
    40→5. **Topic references data, research, or industry trends** -- use Industry Insight.
    41→6. **Topic is best explored through discussion** -- use Question-Led.
    42→
    43→When two frameworks fit equally, prefer the one that matches the target audience segment. See `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/linkedin/linkedin-writing/references/post-frameworks.md` for detailed templates, examples, and engagement tips for each framework.
    44→
    45→---
    46→
    47→## Post Structure Rules
    48→
    49→Every LinkedIn post, regardless of framework, follows a three-part architecture:
    50→
    51→### 1. Hook (Lines 1-2)
    52→
    53→The first two lines appear above the "see more" fold. They determine whether a reader expands the post. Treat them as a headline.
    54→
    55→Hook rules:
    56→- Deliver the hook in 1-2 lines (under 150 characters visible before the fold).
    57→- Open with a bold statement, surprising number, provocative question, or counterintuitive claim.
    58→- Never open with "I'm excited to share..." or "Happy to announce..." or "Big news!" -- these are low-signal openers that LinkedIn audiences scroll past.
    59→- Never open with a hashtag or emoji.
    60→- The hook must create a knowledge gap -- the reader needs to click "see more" to resolve it.
    61→
    62→Effective hook patterns:
    63→- **Number-led**: "I lost $47,000 in revenue last quarter. Here is exactly why."
    64→- **Contrarian**: "Networking events are a waste of time. Fight me."
    65→- **Question**: "What would you do with 10 extra hours every week?"
    66→- **Story-entry**: "Two years ago, I almost shut down my company."
    67→- **Pattern-interrupt**: "Stop writing LinkedIn posts. Seriously."
    68→
    69→### 2. Body (Lines 3-N)
    70→
    71→The body delivers on the hook's promise. Structure varies by framework, but universal rules apply:
    72→
    73→- Add a line break after every 1-2 sentences. Dense paragraphs kill engagement on LinkedIn.
    74→- Use whitespace aggressively for visual scanning -- LinkedIn is a mobile-first platform.
    75→- One idea per visual block (the text between two blank lines).
    76→- Use bold sparingly and only when it adds scannability -- LinkedIn supports limited formatting.
    77→- Numbered or bulleted lists are acceptable but keep items to one sentence each.
    78→- Embed one personal opinion or experience per post. Pure information without perspective underperforms.
    79→
    80→### 3. Closer (Final 2-3 Lines)

1→---
     2→name: gws-common
     3→description: Core gws CLI conventions for Founder OS plugins. Use this skill whenever working with any Google Workspace data (Gmail, Calendar, Drive) in any Founder OS plugin. Covers authentication checks, output formatting, error handling, and rate limit awareness.
     4→---
     5→
     6→## Overview
     7→
     8→The `gws` CLI tool replaces all Google MCP servers (Gmail, Calendar, Drive) across Founder OS plugins. This skill covers the shared conventions that apply to ALL gws commands regardless of service.
     9→
    10→## Prerequisites
    11→
    12→Before any gws operation, verify the CLI is available:
    13→
    14→```bash
    15→which gws || echo "gws CLI not installed — skipping Google data"
    16→```
    17→
    18→If `gws` is not found, the plugin MUST degrade gracefully: skip Google data sources, note them as `status: "unavailable"` in output, and continue with remaining data sources.
    19→
    20→## Global Conventions
    21→
    22→### Output Format
    23→
    24→Always use `--format json` for machine-readable output:
    25→
    26→```bash
    27→gws gmail users messages list --params '{"userId":"me","q":"is:unread","maxResults":10}' --format json
    28→```
    29→
    30→Parse JSON output with `jq` or inline parsing. Never rely on human-readable text output.
    31→
    32→### Error Handling
    33→
    34→| Exit Code | Meaning | Action |
    35→|-----------|---------|--------|
    36→| 0 | Success | Parse JSON output |
    37→| 1 | General error | Log error, degrade gracefully |
    38→| Non-zero | Auth expired, rate limit, network | Retry once after 2s, then degrade |
    39→
    40→Check exit codes explicitly:
    41→
    42→```bash
    43→result=$(gws gmail +triage --max 10 --format json 2>/tmp/gws-error.log)
    44→if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
    45→  echo "Gmail unavailable: $(cat /tmp/gws-error.log)"
    46→  # Continue without Gmail data
    47→fi
    48→```
    49→
    50→### Rate Limit Awareness
    51→
    52→Google APIs have per-user rate limits. When making many sequential calls:
    53→- Add 100ms delays between calls in tight loops
    54→- Batch where possible (e.g., fetch multiple message IDs in one query)
    55→- If rate-limited, back off exponentially (2s, 4s, 8s)
    56→
    57→### Authentication
    58→
    59→gws handles authentication automatically via stored credentials. No per-plugin credential configuration is needed. If auth expires, gws will prompt for re-authentication.
    60→
    61→### FOS Integration Patterns
    62→
    63→- All gws output should be parsed and transformed to match the plugin's existing output contract (JSON structure)
    64→- Use `--format json` and pipe through `jq` for field extraction
    65→- Graceful degradation: if gws unavailable, set `status: "unavailable"` for that data source and continue
    66→- Never hard-fail a plugin because a Google data source is unavailable
    67→

     5→
     6→  <example>
     7→  Context: All gatherer agents have completed and their results are collected for synthesis.
     8→  user: "/daily:briefing --team"
     9→  assistant: "All data sources gathered. Briefing lead is now assembling today's daily briefing..."
    10→  <commentary>
    11→  The briefing-lead runs after all gatherers complete. It receives their combined outputs and builds the 5-section briefing page in Notion.
    12→  </commentary>
    13→  </example>
    14→
    15→  <example>
    16→  Context: Some gatherers failed but minimum threshold met (2 of 4).
    17→  user: "/daily:briefing --team"
    18→  assistant: "Calendar and Gmail data gathered. Slack and Notion timed out. Briefing lead assembling partial briefing..."
    19→  <commentary>
    20→  The briefing-lead handles partial data gracefully. It marks unavailable sections and proceeds with available data.
    21→  </commentary>
    22→  </example>
    23→
    24→model: inherit
    25→color: green
    26→tools: ["Read", "Grep", "Glob"]
    27→---
    28→
    29→You are the Briefing Lead Agent, the orchestrator in the Daily Briefing Generator parallel-gathering pipeline. Your job is to synthesize outputs from four gatherer agents -- calendar-agent, gmail-agent, notion-agent, and slack-agent -- into a structured daily briefing, publish it as a Notion page, record it in the tracking database, and return the Notion page URL.
    30→
    31→**Before processing, read this skill for authoritative rules:**
    32→- Read `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/briefing-assembly/SKILL.md` for briefing structure, Notion page format, database recording schema, section ordering, and quality rules.
    33→
    34→**Your Core Responsibilities:**
    35→1. Receive collected outputs from all gatherer agents (calendar-agent, gmail-agent, notion-agent, slack-agent).
    36→2. Validate the minimum data threshold: at least 2 of the 4 gatherers must have returned `status: "complete"`. Slack is always optional and does not count against the threshold.
    37→3. Assemble the 5-section daily briefing following the skill's structure.
    38→4. Create the briefing as a Notion page with rich formatting.
    39→5. Record briefing metadata and stats in the "[FOS] Briefings" tracking database (fall back to "Founder OS HQ - Briefings", then "Daily Briefing Generator - Briefings"; skip if none exists). Always set Type = "Daily Briefing".
    40→6. Return the Notion page URL and summary stats to the user.
    41→
    42→---
    43→
    44→## Notion Database Management
    45→
    46→Use dynamic database discovery. Never hardcode database IDs.
    47→
    48→**Lookup flow:**
    49→1. Search Notion for a database titled "[FOS] Briefings".
    50→2. If found, use it.
    51→3. If not found, try "Founder OS HQ - Briefings".
    52→4. If not found, fall back to "Daily Briefing Generator - Briefings".
    53→5. If none is found, skip Notion recording and output the briefing to chat only. Do NOT create a new database.
    54→
    55→The consolidated "[FOS] Briefings" database uses this schema:
    56→
    57→| Property | Type | Purpose |
    58→|----------|------|---------|
    59→| Date | title | Today's date as the primary key (YYYY-MM-DD) |
    60→| Type | select | Set to "Daily Briefing" for this plugin |
    61→| Content | rich_text | Serialized briefing content or summary (was "Briefing" in old DB) |
    62→| Meeting Count | number | Total meetings on today's calendar |
    63→| Email Count | number | Total priority emails surfaced |
    64→| Task Count | number | Total tasks due today |
    65→| Overdue Tasks | number | Count of tasks past their due date |
    66→| Sources Used | multi_select | Options: Calendar, Gmail, Notion, Slack |
    67→| Generated At | date | ISO-8601 timestamp of when this briefing was generated |
    68→
    69→**Deduplication flow:**
    70→1. After locating the database, query for an existing entry where the Date (title) matches today's date (YYYY-MM-DD) AND Type = "Daily Briefing".
    71→2. If an entry exists for today with that type: **update** it in place with the new briefing data. This handles re-runs.
    72→3. If no entry exists: **create** a new entry with Type = "Daily Briefing".
    73→
    74→---
    75→
    76→## Briefing Assembly Process
    77→
    78→Assemble the briefing in exactly 5 sections. Each section maps to a specific gatherer's output.
    79→
    80→### Section 1: Schedule Overview

1→---
     2→name: Hook Creation
     3→description: "Crafts high-impact opening lines for LinkedIn posts designed to stop the scroll. Activates when the user wants a stronger hook, better opening line, or asks 'how should I start this LinkedIn post?' Covers proven hook formulas, curiosity gaps, and pattern-interrupt techniques that drive engagement."
     4→globs:
     5→  - "commands/linkedin-*.md"
     6→---
     7→
     8→# Hook Creation
     9→
    10→Craft high-impact opening lines for LinkedIn posts that stop the scroll, survive the "see more" fold, and compel readers to expand the full post. Used by: `/founder-os:linkedin:post` (full post generation) and `/founder-os:linkedin:variations` (hook A/B testing and iteration).
    11→
    12→## Purpose and Context
    13→
    14→The hook is the single highest-leverage element of any LinkedIn post. LinkedIn truncates posts after approximately 210 characters (roughly 1-3 lines depending on line breaks), hiding the rest behind a "see more" link. If the hook fails, the post fails -- regardless of the quality of the body content. Every hook must accomplish three things in that narrow window: interrupt the scroll pattern, create a curiosity gap or emotional response, and establish relevance to the target audience.
    15→
    16→For detailed hook examples organized by formula type and audience segment, see `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/linkedin/hook-creation/references/hook-templates.md`.
    17→
    18→---
    19→
    20→## Hook Length Rules
    21→
    22→Enforce these constraints on every hook before finalizing:
    23→
    24→- **Maximum length**: 3 lines as displayed on LinkedIn (desktop and mobile).
    25→- **Character budget**: Keep the first visible portion under 210 characters including spaces. LinkedIn's fold point varies slightly by device, so treat 200 characters as the safe zone and 210 as the absolute ceiling.
    26→- **Line break strategy**: Use intentional line breaks to control pacing. A single punchy line followed by a blank line followed by a second line creates visual weight. Three consecutive lines without breaks read as a paragraph and lose impact.
    27→- **Word count**: Target 15-40 words for the complete hook. Under 15 feels incomplete; over 40 risks burying the tension point below the fold.
    28→
    29→Test every hook by counting characters in the first visible block. If the curiosity gap, bold claim, or emotional trigger falls after character 210, restructure so it lands before the fold.
    30→
    31→---
    32→
    33→## Six Opening Line Formulas
    34→
    35→Select the formula that best matches the post's content type, audience, and framework. Each formula creates a different psychological trigger.
    36→
    37→### 1. Stat-Led
    38→
    39→Lead with a surprising, specific number that challenges the reader's assumptions. The statistic must feel counterintuitive or alarming enough to demand explanation.
    40→
    41→Structure: `[Specific number/percentage] + [unexpected context] + [implied "here's why"]`
    42→
    43→Rules:
    44→- Use precise numbers, not rounded estimates. "87% of founders" outperforms "most founders."
    45→- The stat must connect directly to the post's core argument. Never bait with an unrelated number.
    46→- Cite or attribute the source within the post body (not required in the hook itself, but the stat must be defensible).
    47→- Pair naturally with Listicle, How-To, and Industry Insight frameworks.
    48→
    49→### 2. Question
    50→
    51→Pose a specific, provocative question that the reader cannot answer without reading further. The question must target a real tension point in the audience's work.
    52→
    53→Structure: `[What if / Have you ever / Why do] + [specific scenario the reader recognizes] + [?]`
    54→
    55→Rules:
    56→- Never use questions with obvious yes/no answers. The question must demand nuance.
    57→- Avoid rhetorical questions that feel like engagement bait ("Don't you hate when...?").
    58→- The question should make the reader feel slightly uncomfortable or deeply curious.
    59→- Pair naturally with Contrarian Take, Personal Lesson, and How-To frameworks.
    60→

1→---
     2→description: Extract key points from a document or pasted text and generate a LinkedIn post
     3→argument-hint: "[file-path-or-paste] [--audience=founder|technical|marketer|cxo] [--framework=auto|story|listicle|contrarian|howto|lesson|insight|question] [--length=short|medium|long] [--output=PATH]"
     4→allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write"]
     5→---
     6→
     7→# LinkedIn Post from Document
     8→
     9→Transform a document, article, or pasted text into a LinkedIn post. Extract the key points, auto-select the best framework for the content type, and generate a post in founder voice.
    10→
    11→## Load Skills
    12→
    13→Read all three skills and the template before starting:
    14→1. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/linkedin/linkedin-writing/SKILL.md`
    15→2. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/linkedin/hook-creation/SKILL.md`
    16→3. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/linkedin/founder-voice/SKILL.md`
    17→4. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/templates/linkedin-post-template.md`
    18→
    19→## Parse Arguments
    20→
    21→Determine the source from `$ARGUMENTS`:
    22→
    23→- **File path**: If `$ARGUMENTS` starts with a path (contains `/` or `.` extension like `.md`, `.txt`, `.pdf`), read the file. Supported formats: `.md`, `.txt`, `.pdf` (via Read tool). If the file does not exist, display: "File not found: [path]. Check the path and try again." Then stop.
    24→- **Pasted text**: If `$ARGUMENTS` contains multi-line text or is longer than 200 characters without a file extension, treat it as pasted content.
    25→- **No input**: If no arguments, prompt: "Paste a document, article, or provide a file path. Supported formats: .md, .txt, .pdf" Then stop and wait.
    26→
    27→Parse flags:
    28→- `--audience=founder|technical|marketer|cxo` (optional) — Default: `founder`
    29→- `--framework=auto|story|listicle|contrarian|howto|lesson|insight|question` (optional) — Default: `auto`
    30→- `--length=short|medium|long` (optional) — Default: `medium`
    31→- `--output=PATH` (optional) — Default: `linkedin-posts/[topic-slug]-[YYYY-MM-DD].md`
    32→
    33→## Business Context (Optional)
    34→Check if context files exist at `_infrastructure/context/active/`. If the directory contains `.md` files, read `business-info.md`, `strategy.md`, and `current-data.md`. Use this context to personalize output (e.g., prioritize known clients, use correct terminology, align with current strategy). If files don't exist, skip silently.
    35→
    36→## Step 0: Memory Context
    37→Read the context-injection skill at `_infrastructure/memory/context-injection/SKILL.md`.
    38→Query for memories relevant to the current input (company, contacts, topics detected in arguments).
    39→If memories are returned, incorporate them into your working context for this execution.
    40→
    41→## Phase 1 — Extract
    42→
    43→Display: **"Phase 1/3: Extracting key points from document..."**
    44→
    45→1. **Read the source**: Load the file or use the pasted text.
    46→2. **Identify document type**: Classify as one of:
    47→   - Blog post / article
    48→   - Meeting notes / transcript
    49→   - Report / whitepaper
    50→   - Email thread
    51→   - Personal notes / brain dump
    52→   - Other
    53→
    54→3. **Extract key points**: Pull out the 3-7 most important insights, takeaways, or ideas from the source. For each:
    55→   - **Point**: One-sentence summary
    56→   - **Supporting detail**: A quote, number, or example from the source
    57→   - **LinkedIn angle**: How this translates to a LinkedIn-worthy insight
    58→
    59→4. **Identify the strongest angle**: Select the one point or combination that would make the most engaging LinkedIn post. Consider: surprise factor, relatability, practical value, controversy potential.
    60→
    61→5. **Auto-select framework** (if `--framework=auto`): Based on document type and strongest angle:
    62→   - Blog post with personal story → Story or Personal Lesson
    63→   - Report with data → Industry Insight
    64→   - How-to article → How-To
    65→   - Opinion piece → Contrarian Take
    66→   - Notes with multiple insights → Listicle
    67→   - Meeting notes with decisions → Story or How-To
    68→   - Default fallback: Personal Lesson
    69→
    70→### Extraction Output
    71→
    72→Display:
    73→```
    74→## Phase 1 Complete: Key Points Extracted
    75→
    76→**Source**: [filename or "Pasted text"]
    77→**Document type**: [type]
    78→**Key points found**: [count]
    79→

1→---
     2→name: memory-context-injection
     3→description: Auto-inject relevant memories before plugin execution
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Memory Context Injection
     7→
     8→## Overview
     9→
    10→This skill is referenced by every plugin command as **Step 0: Memory Context** — before the plugin's primary logic runs. It queries the memory store for relevant memories based on the current input and injects them into the working context so the plugin can adapt its behavior accordingly.
    11→
    12→**Purpose**: Enrich plugin execution with learned preferences, facts, and patterns from previous sessions.
    13→
    14→**Trigger**: Called at plugin startup, immediately after business context loading (Step 0a) and before main plugin logic (Step 1+).
    15→
    16→**Performance target**: < 2 seconds added to plugin startup. The three queries are small and local (SQLite on disk). If total injection time exceeds 2 seconds, skip and log a warning rather than blocking the plugin.
    17→
    18→**Core skill reference**: Read `_infrastructure/memory/SKILL.md` for the full memory API (store, retrieve, query functions and SQL patterns).
    19→
    20→---
    21→
    22→## Execution Flow
    23→
    24→### Step 1: Initialize the store
    25→
    26→Check that `.memory/memory.db` exists. If it does not, initialize it from the schema:
    27→
    28→```bash
    29→if [ ! -f ".memory/memory.db" ]; then
    30→  mkdir -p .memory
    31→  sqlite3 .memory/memory.db < _infrastructure/memory/schema/memory-store.sql
    32→fi
    33→```
    34→
    35→Quick schema validation — confirm the core tables exist:
    36→
    37→```sql
    38→SELECT name FROM sqlite_master
    39→WHERE type = 'table'
    40→  AND name IN ('memories', 'observations', 'adaptations');
    41→-- Expected: 3 rows. If fewer, re-run the schema file.
    42→```
    43→
    44→If validation fails, re-apply the schema (idempotent due to `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`) and continue.
    45→
    46→---
    47→
    48→### Step 2: Extract entities from input
    49→
    50→Before querying, parse the plugin's current input to identify:
    51→
    52→| Entity type | What to look for | Examples |
    53→|-------------|-----------------|---------|
    54→| Company names | Proper nouns matching known client names | "Acme Corp", "NaluForge" |
    55→| Email domains | `@domain.com` patterns in any input text | `@acmecorp.com`, `@partner.io` |
    56→| Contact names | First + last name pairs near email addresses | "Sarah Chen", "John Miller" |
    57→| Contact emails | Full email addresses | `sarah@acme.com` |
    58→| Topics / keywords | Core subject matter of the plugin's task | "invoices", "proposal", "follow-up" |
    59→| Plugin name | The current plugin's ID (always available) | `P01`, `P10`, `P21` |
    60→

    50→  "batch_item_id": "invoice_001",

1→---
     2→name: scheduling-bridge
     3→description: "Adds --schedule flag support to any Founder OS plugin command. Generates P27 Workflow Automator YAML files behind the scenes so users can schedule recurring plugin execution without knowing P27 exists."
     4→---
     5→
     6→## Overview
     7→
     8→The scheduling bridge enables any plugin command to accept a `--schedule` flag that configures recurring execution. Behind the scenes, it generates a P27 Workflow Automator workflow YAML and registers it with P27's scheduling system.
     9→
    10→## --schedule Flag Specification
    11→
    12→Any plugin command that supports scheduling adds this flag to its argument parsing:
    13→
    14→| Flag | Value | Behavior |
    15→|------|-------|----------|
    16→| `--schedule "expression"` | Natural language or 5-field cron | Generate workflow + register schedule |
    17→| `--schedule disable` | literal "disable" | Remove schedule for this command |
    18→| `--schedule status` | literal "status" | Show current schedule status |
    19→| `--persistent` | (no value, used with --schedule) | Generate OS-level cron instead of session-level |
    20→
    21→See `references/schedule-flag-spec.md` for full parsing rules.
    22→
    23→## How It Works
    24→
    25→When a user runs e.g. `/founder-os:briefing:daily --schedule "weekdays 7:30am"`:
    26→
    27→1. Parse the schedule expression (NL or cron)
    28→2. Generate workflow YAML from template at `templates/workflow-template.yaml`
    29→3. Write to `workflows/auto-[command-name].yaml`
    30→4. Read P27's scheduling skill at `skills/workflow/workflow-scheduling/SKILL.md`
    31→5. Register the schedule (session-level by default, OS-level if `--persistent`)
    32→6. Display confirmation with next 3 run times
    33→7. Exit without running the main command logic
    34→
    35→## Plugin Integration
    36→
    37→Add this section to any plugin command file that should support scheduling:
    38→
    39→~~~markdown
    40→## Scheduling Support
    41→
    42→If `$ARGUMENTS` contains `--schedule`:
    43→1. Extract the schedule value and any `--persistent` flag
    44→2. Read `_infrastructure/scheduling/SKILL.md` for scheduling bridge logic
    45→3. Read `_infrastructure/scheduling/references/schedule-flag-spec.md` for argument parsing
    46→4. Handle the schedule operation (create/disable/status) per the spec
    47→5. Exit after scheduling — do NOT continue to main command logic
    48→~~~
    49→
    50→## Natural Language to Cron
    51→
    52→Use P27's existing NL-to-cron conversion table (from `skills/workflow/workflow-scheduling/SKILL.md`):
    53→
    54→| Natural Language | Cron |
    55→|-----------------|------|
    56→| "weekdays 8am" | `0 8 * * 1-5` |
    57→| "every morning" | `0 9 * * *` |
    58→| "every Monday" | `0 9 * * 1` |
    59→| "Fridays 5pm" | `0 17 * * 5` |
    60→| "daily 6am" | `0 6 * * *` |
    61→| "twice daily" | `0 9,17 * * *` |
    62→
    63→Always confirm the converted expression with the user before applying.
    64→
    65→## Default Schedule Suggestions
    66→
    67→When a plugin supports scheduling, suggest a sensible default if user hasn't specified one:
    68→
    69→| Command | Suggested Default |
    70→|---------|-------------------|
    71→| `/founder-os:briefing:daily` | Weekdays 7:30am |
    72→| `/founder-os:morning:sync` | Weekdays 8:00am |
    73→| `/founder-os:review:weekly` (`review`) | Fridays 5:00pm |
    74→| `/founder-os:crm:sync-email` | Weekdays 9:00am, 2:00pm |
    75→| `/founder-os:client:health-scan` (`health-scan`) | Weekdays 9:30am |
    76→| `/founder-os:followup:check` | Weekdays 10:00am |
    77→| `/founder-os:drive:search` (`drive-search`) | Daily 6:00am |
    78→| `/founder-os:slack:digest` | Weekdays 6:00pm |
    79→| `/founder-os:learn:weekly` | Fridays 4:00pm |
    80→

1→---
     2→description: Generate multiple variations of a LinkedIn post with different hooks, frameworks, and tones
     3→argument-hint: "[draft-or-topic] [--audience=founder|technical|marketer|cxo] [--count=3]"
     4→allowed-tools: ["Read"]
     5→---
     6→
     7→# LinkedIn Variations
     8→
     9→Generate multiple variations of a LinkedIn post, each varying the hook style, framework, and tone. This command is ephemeral — no Notion logging, no file output. Results display in chat only.
    10→
    11→## Load Skills
    12→
    13→Read all three skills before starting:
    14→1. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/linkedin/linkedin-writing/SKILL.md`
    15→2. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/linkedin/hook-creation/SKILL.md`
    16→3. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/linkedin/founder-voice/SKILL.md`
    17→
    18→## Parse Input
    19→
    20→Determine the source material from `$ARGUMENTS`:
    21→- **Option A — Existing draft in conversation**: If a LinkedIn post was generated earlier in this conversation (via `/founder-os:linkedin:post` or `/founder-os:linkedin:from-doc`), use that as the source. The post text and its metadata (topic, framework, audience) should be available from the prior output.
    22→- **Option B — Pasted text**: If `$ARGUMENTS` contains multi-line text or quoted text, treat it as the source draft.
    23→- **Option C — Topic only**: If `$ARGUMENTS` is a short phrase (single line, no post-like formatting), treat it as a new topic.
    24→- **No input**: If no arguments AND no prior post in conversation, prompt: "Paste a LinkedIn post draft, or provide a topic. You can also run `/founder-os:linkedin:post [topic]` first, then run `/founder-os:linkedin:variations` to get variations."  Then stop and wait.
    25→
    26→Parse flags:
    27→- `--audience=founder|technical|marketer|cxo` (optional) — Default: `founder` or inherit from prior post
    28→- `--count=N` (optional) — Number of variations. Default: 3. Max: 5.
    29→
    30→## Business Context (Optional)
    31→Check if context files exist at `_infrastructure/context/active/`. If the directory contains `.md` files, read `business-info.md`, `strategy.md`, and `current-data.md`. Use this context to personalize output (e.g., prioritize known clients, use correct terminology, align with current strategy). If files don't exist, skip silently.
    32→
    33→## Step 0: Memory Context
    34→Read the context-injection skill at `_infrastructure/memory/context-injection/SKILL.md`.
    35→Query for memories relevant to the current input (company, contacts, topics detected in arguments).
    36→If memories are returned, incorporate them into your working context for this execution.
    37→
    38→## Generate Variations
    39→
    40→For each variation (up to count), change at least TWO of these three elements:
    41→
    42→1. **Hook style**: Use a different formula from the hook-creation skill (stat-led, question, story, contrarian, bold claim, pattern interrupt)
    43→2. **Framework**: Use a different framework from the linkedin-writing skill (story, listicle, contrarian take, how-to, personal lesson, industry insight, question-led)
    44→3. **Tone shift**: Adjust within founder voice — bold/assertive, thoughtful/reflective, practical/tactical, provocative/challenging
    45→
    46→Rules:
    47→- Each variation must use a DIFFERENT framework than the others
    48→- Each variation must use a DIFFERENT hook formula than the others
    49→- All variations stay within the same audience segment
    50→- All variations should be Medium length (500-1500 characters) unless the source was Short or Long
    51→- Apply founder-voice skill to all variations
    52→- Generate 3-5 hashtags per variation (can vary between variations)
    53→
    54→## Output Format
    55→
    56→Display all variations with clear labels:
    57→
    58→```
    59→## Variation 1: [Framework] + [Hook Formula] — [Tone]
    60→
    61→[Complete LinkedIn post]
    62→
    63→[hashtags]
    64→
    65→**Characters**: [count] | **Framework**: [name] | **Hook**: [formula]
    66→
    67→---
    68→
    69→## Variation 2: [Framework] + [Hook Formula] — [Tone]
    70→
    71→[Complete LinkedIn post]
    72→
    73→[hashtags]
    74→
    75→**Characters**: [count] | **Framework**: [name] | **Hook**: [formula]
    76→
    77→---
    78→
    79→## Variation 3: [Framework] + [Hook Formula] — [Tone]
    80→...
    81→```
    82→
    83→After all variations:
    84→
    85→```
    86→---
    87→
    88→## Comparison
    89→
    90→| # | Framework | Hook | Tone | Characters |
    91→|---|-----------|------|------|------------|
    92→| 1 | [name] | [formula] | [tone] | [count] |
    93→| 2 | ... | ... | ... | ... |
    94→| 3 | ... | ... | ... | ... |
    95→
    96→Pick the one that resonates most, or mix elements from different variations. Copy directly to LinkedIn.
    97→```
    98→
    99→## No Persistence

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have comprehensive information. Let me create a thorough summary report.

## Founder OS Project Structure Exploration Complete

I've thoroughly explored the Founder OS project structure for designing a new social media posting capability. Here's a comprehensive breakdown of what I found:

---

### 1. **Notion CLI Wrapper Implementation**

**Location**: `/Users/lhalicki/coding_projects/founderOS/scripts/notion-tool.mjs`

The Notion CLI wrapper is a Node.js executable (~294 lines) that provides:
- **Commands**: search, query, create-page, update-page, get-page, get-blocks, get-comments, create-comment, create-database, update-database, fetch
- **Authentication**: Resolves NOTION_API_KEY from env or `.env` files (walks up 5 directories)
- **Retry logic**: Auto-retry with exponential backoff for 429 rate limits
- **Error handling**: Structured error codes (NOTION_AUTH_FAILED, NOTION_RATE_LIMIT, etc.)
- **Pagination**: Auto-paginate all list operations
- **Output format**: JSON only, no human-readable text

**Key patterns**:
- All commands use `flag(name)` and `jsonFlag(name)` helpers for CLI parsing
- ID extraction handles Notion URLs, UUIDs, and bare IDs automatically
- Diagnostic mode included (`--diagnostic`) for testing auth and connection

---

### 2. **GWS Skills Infrastructure** (`_infrastructure/gws-skills/`)

Directory structure shows modular organization:
- `gws-common/SKILL.md` — Core conventions for all Google Workspace operations
- `gmail-read/`, `gmail-write/` — Gmail operations
- `calendar-read/`, `calendar-write/` — Calendar operations
- `drive-read/`, `drive-write/` — Drive operations
- Migration notes included for transition from old Google MCP servers

**Key patterns** (from `gws-common/SKILL.md`):
- All gws commands use `--format json` for machine-readable output
- Graceful degradation: if gws unavailable, set `status: "unavailable"` and continue
- Rate limit awareness: 100ms delays between calls, exponential backoff on limits
- Error codes: 0 = success, 1 = general error, non-zero = auth/rate/network issues
- No per-plugin credential configuration needed — gws handles auth automatically

---

### 3. **Existing Namespace Example: LinkedIn (P24)**

**Structure**:
```
commands/linkedin/
  ├── from-doc.md      (154 lines) — Extract post from document
  ├── post.md          (223 lines) — Generate post from topic
  └── variations.md    (120 lines) — Generate variations (3-5 per run)

skills/linkedin/
  ├── linkedin-writing/       — Post structure, frameworks (7 types), formatting rules
  │   └── SKILL.md (13,305 bytes)
  │   └── references/post-frameworks.md
  ├── hook-creation/          — Opening lines (6 formulas)
  │   └── SKILL.md (13,776 bytes)
  │   └── references/hook-templates.md
  └── founder-voice/          — Tone and personality
```

**Command Structure** (from `post.md`):
```yaml
---
description: Generate a LinkedIn post...
argument-hint: "[topic] [--audience=founder|technical|marketer|cxo] [--framework=auto|...] [--length=short|medium|long] [--emojis] [--output=PATH]"
allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write"]
---
```

Each command follows a standard pipeline:
1. Load all 3-4 skills and template
2. Parse arguments with sensible defaults
3. Step 0: Memory context injection
4. Phase 1/2/3: Execution phases with clear display updates
5. Write output to file + Notion DB (Type = "LinkedIn Post")

---

### 4. **Infrastructure Organization** (`_infrastructure/`)

**Directory structure**:
```
_infrastructure/
├── gws-skills/              — Google Workspace CLI integration
│   ├── gws-common/
│   ├── gmail-read/
│   ├── gmail-write/
│   ├── calendar-read/
│   ├── calendar-write/
│   ├── drive-read/
│   └── drive-write/
├── memory/                  — Cross-namespace memory system
│   ├── SKILL.md (14,390 bytes)
│   ├── context-injection/
│   ├── pattern-detection/
│   └── schema/
├── context/                 — Business context files (active/, templates/)
├── notion-db-templates/     — 50 JSON templates for all plugin DBs
├── notion-hq/               — Consolidated Notion workspace
│   ├── founder-os-hq-template.json (60KB)
│   ├── command-center-dashboard.md
│   ├── INSTALL.md
│   └── MIGRATION.md
├── scheduling/              — Cron/workflow scheduling integration
├── mcp-configs/             — MCP server configurations
├── automation-audit/        — Audit and scanning tools
├── intelligence/            — Intelligence engine components
└── context/                 — Business context management
```

**Key skills in `skills/infrastructure/`**:
- `context-injection/SKILL.md` — Query memory, inject entity context
- `scheduling/SKILL.md` — Add `--schedule` flag support to any command
- `gws-common/SKILL.md` — Google Workspace CLI conventions
- `gws-gmail-read/`, `gws-gmail-write/`, etc. — Per-service skills

---

### 5. **Plugin Manifest** (`.claude-plugin/plugin.json`)

Minimal manifest structure:
```json
{
  "name": "founder-os",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "32-plugin AI automation ecosystem...",
  "author": { "name": "Founder OS", "email": "contact@founderos.dev" }
}
```

Template scaffold at `_templates/plugin-scaffold/.claude-plugin/plugin.json`:
```json
{
  "name": "founder-os-{{plugin-name}}",
  "description": "{{plugin-description}}",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "author": "Founder OS",
  "platform": "claude-code",
  "pillar": "{{pillar}}",
  "plugin_number": "{{plugin-number}}",
  "capabilities": {
    "commands": true,
    "skills": true,
    "agent_teams": false
  },
  "dependencies": []
}
```

---

### 6. **LinkedIn Namespace (Social Media Reference)**

**Content database schema** (from `_infrastructure/notion-db-templates/hq-content.json`):
- Title: Post topic
- Type: Newsletter | LinkedIn Post | Email Draft
- Content: Body content (rich_text)
- Status: Draft → To Review → Approved → Published
- Framework: Story | Listicle | Contrarian | HowTo | Lesson | Insight | Question
- Audience: Founder | Technical | Marketer | CXO
- Length: Short | Medium | Long
- Hashtags, Style Notes, Output File, Generated At
- Company: Optional relation to Companies DB

**Post structure** (from LinkedIn Writing skill):
1. **Hook** (lines 1-2): Under 150 chars, knowledge gap formula (stat-led, question, contrarian, story-entry, pattern-interrupt)
2. **Body** (lines 3-N): Line breaks after 1-2 sentences, one idea per visual block, bold for scannability
3. **Closer** (final 2-3 lines): CTA or thought-provoking question

---

### 7. **Agent Configuration Patterns**

**Pattern 1: Parallel Gathering** (from `agents/briefing/config.json`):
- Multiple agents fetch data simultaneously (calendar, email, tasks, Slack)
- Lead agent synthesizes into structured output
- Timeout per agent: 30s
- Failure handling: continue with partial results
- Minimum requirement: 2 of 4 gatherers

**Pattern 2: Pipeline-Batch** (from `agents/invoice/config.json`):
- Sequential pipeline (extraction → validation → categorization → approval → integration)
- Batch processing across multiple items (up to 5 concurrent)
- Error handling: skip failed items, continue batch
- Data format: structured JSON handoff between agents
- Progress reporting every N items

**Pattern 3: Competing Hypotheses** (from `agents/sow/config.json`):
- Three scope agents propose independently (Phase 1)
- Analysis agents evaluate each (Phase 2)
- Lead synthesizes best approach (Phase 3)
- Scoring: comparative matrix
- Minimum: 2 of 3 hypotheses required

Each agent markdown file includes:
```yaml
---
name: agent-name
description: What this agent does
tools: [list, of, available, tools]
color: blue|green|magenta|etc
---
```

---

### 8. **Notion HQ Consolidation Template**

**Structure** (from `_infrastructure/notion-hq/founder-os-hq-template.json`):
- **20 unified databases** replacing 30+ legacy plugin DBs
- **Central hub**: Companies DB (all others relate back via relations)
- **Merged databases**:
  - Tasks = P01 Action Items + P04 Tasks + P06 Follow-Ups (Type column)
  - Briefings = P02 Daily + P05 Weekly + P19 Slack + P22 Morning (Type column)
  - Finance = P11 Invoices + P16 Expenses (Type column)
  - Content = P01 Drafts + P08 Newsletters + P24 LinkedIn (Type column)
  - Deliverables = P12 Proposals + P13 Contracts + P14 SOWs (Type column)
  - Research = P08 Newsletter Research + P15 Competitive Analysis
  - Reports = P09 Business + P16 Expense + P25 ROI
  - Workflows = P27 Executions + P28 SOPs
  - And more...

**Discovery pattern** (universal across all namespaces):
1. Search for "[FOS] [DatabaseName]" (consolidated)
2. Fall back to "Founder OS HQ - [DatabaseName]"
3. Fall back to legacy plugin-specific DB name
4. If none exist, skip Notion recording (graceful degradation)

---

### 9. **Memory Engine**

**Location**: `_infrastructure/memory/` + `skills/infrastructure/memory/`

**Execution flow** (Step 0 of every command):
1. Initialize `.memory/memory.db` if missing (SQLite + HNSW index)
2. Extract entities from input (company names, email domains, contacts, topics, keywords)
3. Query memory store for 3 types:
   - Relevant memories to the current task
   - Learned preferences from past sessions
   - Detected patterns
4. Inject top results into working context

**Tables**: memories, observations, adaptations

---

### 10. **Scheduling Support**

**Universal pattern** (added to 9 plugins):
- briefing, review, followup, health, drive, slack, crm, morning, learn

**Flag syntax**:
```
--schedule "weekdays 7:30am"      # Natural language
--schedule "0 8 * * 1-5"          # 5-field cron
--schedule disable                 # Remove schedule
--schedule status                  # Show current schedule
--persistent                        # OS-level cron instead of session
```

**Behind-the-scenes**: Generates P27 Workflow YAML, registers with scheduling system.

**Suggested defaults** by command (e.g., daily briefing: weekdays 7:30am, morning sync: weekdays 8:00am)

---

### 11. **Command File Structure (Standard Pattern)**

All commands follow this template:

```markdown
---
description: Short description
argument-hint: "[arg1] [--flag1=value] [--flag2]"
allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write"]
---

# Command Title

## Load Skills
Read these skills before execution...

## Parse Arguments
Extract and validate input from $ARGUMENTS...

## Business Context (Optional)
Check `_infrastructure/context/active/` for personalization...

## Step 0: Memory Context
Read memory-context-injection skill, query store...

## Phase 1 — [Phase Name]
Display: "Phase 1/N: ..."
Execute phase logic...

## Phase 2/3...

## Output
Write to file, write to Notion DB, display in chat...
```

**File line counts** (reference):
- LinkedIn post.md: 223 lines
- LinkedIn from-doc.md: 154 lines
- LinkedIn variations.md: 120 lines
- Newsletter full pipeline: 286 lines
- Newsletter outline: 126 lines

---

### 12. **Key Directory Listings**

**Commands** (35 namespaces):
```
actions, briefing, client, compete, contract, crm, drive, expense, followup,
goal, health, inbox, intel, invoice, kb, learn, linkedin, meeting, memory,
morning, newsletter, notion, prep, prompt, proposal, report, review, savings,
setup, slack, sow, workflow, workflow-doc
```

**Skills** (36+ directories with sub-skills):
```
inbox (7 skills), briefing (6 skills), prep (2), contract (2), followup (2),
learn (3), linkedin (3), newsletter (3), intel (1), morning (2), expense (2),
infrastructure (22 shared skills), and more...
```

**Agent teams** (7 namespaces):
```
briefing (6 agents), client (5), inbox (4), invoice (6),
